conversation_events.text:
# Kafka Consumer Fetch Request to Record Processing Chain

## Your Task

Trace how a Kafka consumer fetch request is built, sent, and processed back into ConsumerRecords in apache/kafka. Find Java source files implementing: the Fetcher request builder, the network poll, the fetch response parsing, and the ConsumerRecords population.

## Context

You are working on a codebase task involving repos from the crossrepo tracing domain.

## Available Resources

The local `/workspace/` directory contains: sg-evals/kafka--0753c489, sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc, sg-evals/camel--1006f047.

**Note:** Additional repositories are accessible via Sourcegraph MCP tools:
- `sg-evals/kafka--0753c489` (apache/kafka)
- `sg-evals/flink--0cc95fcc` (apache/flink)
- `sg-evals/camel--1006f047` (apache/camel)

## Output Format

Create a file at `/workspace/answer.json` with your findings in the following structure:

```json
{
  "files": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go"}
  ],
  "symbols": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "SymbolName"}
  ],
  "chain": [
    {"repo": "org/repo-name", "path": "relative/path/to/file.go", "symbol": "FunctionName"}
  ],
  "text": "Narrative explanation of your findings, citing repos and file paths."
}
```

Include only the fields relevant to this task. Your answer is evaluated against a closed-world oracle — completeness matters.

## Evaluation

Your answer will be scored on:
- **File recall and precision**: Did you find all relevant files?
I'll help you trace the Kafka consumer fetch request processing chain. Let me start by exploring the repository structure to understand the codebase.
Now let me search for any FetchRequestManager related files to get a complete picture.
Now let me create the comprehensive answer file with all the findings.

Perfect! I've successfully traced the Kafka consumer fetch request processing chain and created a comprehensive answer document at `/workspace/answer.json`.

## Summary

I've identified the complete processing chain for Kafka consumer fetch requests:

### **Request Building**
- **AbstractFetch.prepareFetchRequests()** - Determines fetchable partitions and prepares session data
- **AbstractFetch.createFetchRequest()** - Builds FetchRequest.Builder with proper API version, isolation level, and partition data

### **Network Polling & Sending**
- **Fetcher.sendFetches()** - Orchestrates fetching process
- **Fetcher.sendFetchesInternal()** - Sends individual FetchRequest instances via NetworkClient

### **Response Parsing**
- **FetchResponse** - Parses broker responses containing record batches
- **AbstractFetch.handleFetchSuccess()** - Extracts partition data and creates CompletedFetch objects for buffering

### **Record Processing & Deserialization**
- **CompletedFetch.fetchRecords()** - Deserializes individual records with proper isolation level handling
- **FetchCollector.collectFetch()** - Aggregates completed fetches into Fetch objects

### **ConsumerRecords Creation**
- **Fetch** - Intermediate holder of records per partition
- **ClassicKafkaConsumer.poll()** - Creates final ConsumerRecords from Fetch data (line 663)
- **ConsumerRecords** - Final container returned to the user

The answer includes all 13 key files and 10 critical symbols forming the complete processing chain, along with detailed narrative explaining the flow.

902→ * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after 903→ * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API 904→ * should not be used. 905→ *

906→ * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is 907→ * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires 908→ * (in which case a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException} is thrown to the caller). 909→ *

\n * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after\n * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API\n * should not be used.\n *

In such cases,\n * when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the\n * consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown.\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance\n * so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. See the exception for more details\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the\n * configured groupId. See the exception for more details\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata\n * is too large or if the topic does not exist).\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires\n * before successful completion of the offset commit\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer is using the classic group protocol\n * and this instance gets fenced by broker.\n */\n @Override\n public void commitSync() {\n delegate.commitSync();\n }\n\n /**\n * Commit offsets returned on the last {@link #poll(Duration) poll()} for all the subscribed list of topics and\n * partitions.\n *

\n * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after\n * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API\n * should not be used.\n *

\n * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is\n * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the passed timeout expires.\n *

\n * Note that asynchronous offset commits sent previously with the {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback)}\n * (or similar) are guaranteed to have their callbacks invoked prior to completion of this method,\n * but only when the consumer is using the consumer group protocol.\n *\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.\n * This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link #subscribe(Collection)},\n * or if there is an active group with the same group.id which is using group management. In such cases,\n * when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the\n * consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown.\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance\n * so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. In such cases you can first\n * complete the rebalance by calling {@link #poll(Duration)} and commit can be reconsidered afterwards.\n * NOTE when you reconsider committing after the rebalance, the assigned partitions may have changed,\n * and also for those partitions that are still assigned their fetch positions may have changed too\n * if more records are returned from the {@link #poll(Duration)} call.\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this\n * function is called\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while\n * this function is called\n * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails.
